pr_description:
This PR contains a:

- [ ] **bugfix**
- [x] new **feature**
- [ ] **code refactor**
- [x] **test update** <!-- if bug or feature is checked, this should be too -->
- [ ] **typo fix**
- [ ] **metadata update**

### Motivation / Use-Case

We use the `file-loader` in a [next.js plugin](https://gist.github.com/fabb/cdaad150e79a1dc78aa97fc315d40cb7) to automatically generate asset urls by importing the assets. The asset file names will be hashed, which is good for caching indefinitely. The plugin supports next.js' `assetPrefix` which allows to provide a different cache server host url for static assets. Next.js [uses `__webpack_public_path__` by default](https://github.com/zeit/next.js/blob/7e7f2c0a6df2cf6a48fa4b34beb1d5befe13fa54/packages/next/client/index.js#L47).

The problem is that in our case the `assetPrefix` is dynamic at runtime, and will be set based on environment variables which are **not present at compile time**. The plugin-static gets used during compile time though, and will only use the `assetPrefix` that is based on environment variables present at compile time. [The proper way around this is to make use of `__webpack_public_path__`](https://webpack.js.org/guides/public-path/#on-the-fly). The problem with `file-loader` is that when setting the `publicPath`, it is [not possible to use the `__webpack_public_path__`](https://github.com/webpack-contrib/file-loader/blob/011cc38d4ed470729ff0fbc6c1d7146896603598/src/index.js#L44). Therefore a new setting is needed which allows to prefix with `__webpack_public_path__`.

### Breaking Changes

No breaking changes.

### Additional Info
